text: The surgeon excises the falciform ligament to create space, then enters the lesser sac, revealing the anatomical structures within.
The surgeon identifies the inferior and superior surfaces of the pancreas, visually distinguishing these anatomical landmarks during the laparoscopic Whipple procedure.
The surgeon orients around the gastrocolic trunk, using the Thunderbeat device to manage the branches effectively.
The surgeon identifies the gastrocolic trunk at its insertion into the superior mesenteric vein, while the tunnel of the portal vein is visible posterior to the pancreatic neck.
The laparoscopic camera provides a clear view as the surgeon carefully dissects the tunnel leading up to the portal vein, maintaining precise control throughout the procedure.
The surgeon clips and divides the gastrocolic trunk, which is visually identifiable as it inserts into the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon addresses bleeding caused by a misplaced clip using the Thunderbeat device, which is then employed to transect the vessel near the gastrocolic trunk.
The surgeon opens the tunnel further, providing a clearer view of the portal vein posterior to the pancreatic neck, assessing the resectability of the surrounding structures.
The surgeon opens the pars flaccida, located posterior to the stomach, revealing underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon opens the duodenum distal to the pylorus, revealing the anatomical structure for further dissection.
The surgeon incorporates the right gastric vessels into the surgical field, preparing for their management during the procedure.
The surgeon attempts to preserve the pylorus, but observes that the pylorus and proximal duodenum appear dusky, leading to their excision back to the stomach at the end of the operation.
The video shows a clean staple line with the pancreas visible in the posterior position relative to the staple line.
The surgeon inserts a Nathanson liver retractor and begins dissection superior to the pancreas, revealing the hepatic artery dissected out from the superior edge of the pancreas.

The surgeon isolates the gastroduodenal artery, using visual differentiation of tissue colors to aid in identification. The vessel is occluded momentarily, confirming a strong pulse into the liver, indicating successful isolation.
The surgeon ties and clips the gastroduodenal artery (GDA) to prevent a pseudoaneurysm, using the ThunderBeat device to perform a triple ligation of the artery.
The surgeon prepares to complete the pancreatic neck tunnel, focusing on the area between the pancreas and surrounding structures.
The surgeon visualizes the pancreatic neck tunnel, with most of the tunnel exposed. The dissection underneath the pancreas is nearly complete.
The surgeon uses a grasper to extend across the pancreatic neck tunnel, indicating the completion of the tunnel's dissection.

The surgeon performs the Coker maneuver, starting inferior to the mesentery, with the duodenum partially mobilized. The inferior vena cava (IVC) becomes visible, and dissection is carried out directly on the IVC for safety.

The surgeon inserts the camera through the right flank, utilizing additional accessory ports for enhanced visualization of the posterior duodenum. The left renal vein is clearly visible as it inserts into the inferior vena cava (IVC).

The surgeon observes the takeoff of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) above the pancreatic neck tunnel, with the pulse visibly transmitting through the adjacent vein. The trocar placement is distant, causing some difficulty in reaching the tissues, but the specimen is being mobilized effectively.
The surgeon opens the ligament of Treitz and uses cautery to detach its attachments, creating a visible window to the left side of the mesentery.
The surgeon identifies a suitable location to divide the small bowel, creates a window through the mesentery, and staples across the bowel from the patient's right side.

The surgeon further mobilizes the mesentery of the small bowel, isolating the uncinate process. The superior mesenteric vein is visible extending towards the pancreatic neck tunnel, with branches of the mesentery and uncinate process being dissected.
The surgeon transects the pancreatic neck using the Thunderbeat device, achieving excellent hemostasis and releasing tension on the specimen.

The surgeon mobilizes the uncinate process, with visible oozy bleeding from the superior mesenteric vein and portal vein side branches. Despite the bleeding, the blood vessels and strandy soft tissues are clearly visualized.

The surgeon exposes the superior mesenteric artery (SMA), which is visibly pulsing, along with small branches of the vein and artery. These branches are either clipped or transected using the Thunderbeat device.
The surgeon uses the Thunder Beat device to manage minor bleeding, allowing the dissection to continue smoothly in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses the Thunder Beat to take residual attachments around the pulsing superior mesenteric artery (SMA), with clips applied as needed. The vascular groove is visibly peeled away, revealing the underlying structures.

The surgeon isolates the bile duct and identifies the remaining attachment of lymphatic tissue to the right branch of the hepatic artery. The gallbladder remains attached at the confluence with the common hepatic duct and is prepared for mobilization and removal.

The surgeon applies lateral traction to the cystic duct, isolating the common bile duct. The common bile duct is then sharply divided just distal to the cystic duct insertion. The duct appears smaller than expected, measuring approximately five millimeters in diameter, with a visibly good blood supply.

The surgeon uses touch cautery to quickly control minor bleeding. The duct appears suitable for an anastomosis, measuring approximately five millimeters in diameter, similar to the tip of the suction device. The surgeon re-enters through the lateral port.
In the lateral view, the surgeon prepares for reconstruction by bringing the bowel up through the retro mesenteric window, ensuring it is carefully oriented to avoid any twisting.

The surgeon positions the bowel and examines the pancreas for the pancreatic duct. Despite good mobility of the pancreas, the duct is not immediately visible. The surgeon uses scissors to sharply cut the transected end of the pancreas in an attempt to identify the duct.
The surgeon attempts to locate an orifice on the pancreas but is unsuccessful. The pancreas is further mobilized in preparation for a pancreaticogastrostomy through an extraction incision.

The surgeon uses the Thunder Beat device to carefully open the bowel, ensuring proper orientation. A corner stay suture with a knot on the outside is placed full thickness using IV-O polysorb, held by the assistant. Another IV-O polysorb suture is used to run a continuous closure of the back wall, which is tied down and run towards the surgeon.

The surgeon ensures the pancreatic duct remains open while completing the back wall of the anastomosis. Using the same suture for the running closure, the suture is brought out through the bile duct side and run back toward the previous corner stitch, maintaining the integrity of the anastomosis.

The surgeon checks the anastomosis using clean white gauze sponges, which are left in place briefly and then removed, showing no evidence of biliary leak. The hepatico-jejunostomy appears intact. A Jackson-Pratt drain is positioned appropriately, and the Nathanson retractor is released.
